Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time job, but with limited resources I often had to study at the library or wherever I could find reliable wifi. I'd usually read through materials and take handwritten notes since that's how I learn best. Some weeks I managed to put in solid hours, other weeks life just got in the way and I'd barely touch anything until something was due.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening. I aimed for consistency but between work shifts and just trying to manage everything on a tight budget, regular times were hard to maintain. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely, cramming reading sessions into whatever gaps I could find. I wished I could be more structured, but reality didn't always cooperate with my plans.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check if something was due or to download reading materials. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would prompt me to go see what I'd missed. I also logged in to watch lecture videos when I had stable internet access, which wasn't always guaranteed at home. It was more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of logging in every couple of days to check announcements and watch videos. It started feeling normal when I realized I was falling behind and needed to catch up. But that routine broke down when things got harder emotionally and I just couldn't muster the energy. The habit never really stuck permanently for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written transcripts were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and go through them with a highlighter, making notes in the margins. The videos were okay but I preferred having text I could re-read at my own pace. Discussion posts occasionally helped me see different perspectives on confusing topics.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional supplementary videos and some of the interactive elements. With limited internet at home, streaming extra content felt like a waste of my data. Some resources just seemed redundant to what was already in the readings, so I prioritized what I thought would directly help my grades. Time and resources were tight, so I had to be selective.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help me pass, I was more likely to engage with it. Clear instructions and obvious relevance made a big difference. Anything that felt vague or disconnected from assessment got pushed to the bottom of my priority list because I just didn't have the luxury of exploring freely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on. Required ones had clear consequences so they got my attention even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I always thought I'd get to it later but later never came. There was this guilt about skipping them, but survival mode meant focusing on what would actually count toward my grade.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and work through them on paper, writing out summaries and key points by hand. I used notebooks constantly since writing helps me remember things. I also borrowed textbooks from friends or the library that weren't tracked anywhere. Probably half my actual learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: Many evenings I'd sit with printed materials or handwritten notes reviewing concepts, and none of that showed up anywhere digital. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I made by hand, or rewrite my notes to consolidate understanding. Sometimes I discussed course topics with classmates in person over coffee. The LMS only saw me when I was downloading or submitting, not when I was actually processing information.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I could, then log out and study offline for days. My internet situation made it impractical to stay connected while studying. I'd watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly without ever watching again. The single access didn't reflect how much I actually engaged with that content.
Q12: There were definitely weeks when engaging became really hard. I was feeling pretty low emotionally, kind of sad and unmotivated about everything. The CBT sessions I attended helped somewhat, but there were still days when opening my laptop felt impossible. Financial stress was always in the background too, making it hard to focus on academics when I was worried about basic expenses.
Q13: Workload from my job and general fatigue affected my LMS use significantly. After long shifts, I'd log in with good intentions but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant the course often got whatever energy was left over, which wasn't much. Confusion about some topics made me avoid those sections entirely because I didn't have the mental bandwidth to struggle through them.
Q14: There were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. I'd be logged in, technically watching something, but my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or just feeling empty. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, going through the motions. Those sessions looked like engagement but were basically me being physically present while mentally absent.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was avoidance when I felt overwhelmed or uncertain about how to approach something. The low motivation made starting anything feel like pushing a boulder uphill. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It's a cycle I recognized but couldn't always break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly find the energy to log in and submit something. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They created urgency that my internal motivation couldn't generate on its own. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a small boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to improve, but other times it just reinforced feeling like I wasn't good enough. When I was already feeling sad, negative comments hit harder and made me less likely to engage with that topic again. I needed feedback but didn't always handle it well emotionally.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework but didn't always match my circumstances. Having materials available to download was essential for my situation. But the pacing assumed consistent engagement that I couldn't always maintain. When modules built on each other, falling behind once made catching up feel overwhelming. More flexibility would have helped someone in my position.
Q19: Having downloadable readings made engagement possible for me given my internet limitations. That was crucial. But some interactive elements required consistent connectivity that I didn't have. The discussion requirements felt awkward because I couldn't always respond in real-time when conversations were happening. Clearer connections between activities and assessments would have helped me prioritize better.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding without finding it, clicking around trying to figure things out. Or they could be dealing with personal stuff that makes completing work impossible even when they're trying. Sometimes high activity is actually anxiety-driven, checking and rechecking without productive output. Life circumstances can make the gap between trying and achieving really wide.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS activity was probably lower than some classmates. A lot of my learning happened offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. If you only looked at my login frequency, you'd think I wasn't trying, but I was studying in ways that just didn't register digitally. The system can't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on. They're probably good at tracking submission timing and basic participation. But they miss all the offline studying, the emotional state behind the clicks, and whether someone is actually understanding anything. They can't tell if someone is engaged or just going through motions while struggling inside.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just completing tasks but thinking critically about concepts and trying to connect them to what I already know. For me, engagement happens most when I'm writing and processing ideas, even if no one can see that happening. It's an internal state as much as external behavior.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied using printed notes and handwritten summaries. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made some weeks nearly impossible or the financial stress constantly in the background. It can't see the conversations with classmates in person or the times I helped others understand concepts. My engagement was real but largely invisible to any tracking system.
